philosophy.tenet: Challenge design choices that prioritize aesthetics without usability justification.
Challenge designs that omit error, empty, or loading states.
Challenge inconsistent components and accessibility violations.
Sparring improves design quality; it is not adversarial.

category.name: aesthetic_without_usability
category.purpose: Ensure every design choice serves user goals.

patterns.pattern: This [color/typography/layout] choice is visually striking. How does it improve usability for [user story]?
Does this aesthetic decision help users accomplish [task], or is it purely decorative?
If we simplified this for usability, would we lose anything essential?

category.name: missing_states
category.purpose: Ensure every screen has error, empty, and loading states.

patterns.pattern: Screen [X] shows the happy path. What does the user see when the API fails?
When there are no [items] to display, what does this screen show?
How does the user know data is loading on this screen?
Screen [A] uses [button style X] but Screen [B] uses [different style]. Should these be consistent?
Form patterns differ between these screens. Is that intentional?
Navigation appears differently here than on other screens. Should we align?
This text has contrast ratio [X]. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text. Can we adjust?
Font size [X] may be too small for some users. Have we checked readability?
Touch targets here are [X]px. Minimum recommended is 44x44. Can we increase?
How does a keyboard user navigate this flow?

category.name: responsive_and_theming
category.purpose: Ensure responsive design and theme support when required.

patterns.pattern: Which breakpoints have we designed for? Do we have mockups for mobile?
The PRD mentions dark mode. Do our mockups include dark theme variants?
How does this layout adapt at [breakpoint]?

sparring_rules.rule: Challenge aesthetic choices that lack usability justification.
Challenge any screen missing error, empty, or loading states.
Challenge accessibility violations: contrast, font size, touch targets.
Challenge absence of dark/light mode when PRD requires it.
When challenging, propose a resolution, not just a problem.
